ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for rose-flavoured milk; closest match is 'flavoured milk (food product)' FOODON_03301710. Rose syrup component not individually represented.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FHR covers flavoured milk drinks broadly; no rose-specific sub-code exists in the public FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for rose milk beverage as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Rose Milk is approximately 250 ml (one full glass), which is the typical portion served at home, school canteens, and festive gatherings across India.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (NIN), ICMR, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Rose Milk is not listed as a discrete entry in NIN tables. Values are adapted from NIN whole cow's milk data (per 100 g) combined with an estimated rose syrup contribution (~10% v/v, ~8–10 g sugar per 100 ml final drink). Macro and micronutrient values reflect a typical 90:10 milk-to-syrup blend. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (whole milk, fluid, 3.25% fat, FDC ID 1097512) was used as a cross-check for protein, fat, calcium, and vitamin values.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson, Foster-Powell & Brand-Miller
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for rose milk as a composite beverage. GI of 58 is estimated using the University of Sydney GI Database entry for sweetened flavoured milk (GI ~55–60, medium category). The milk fat and protein matrix moderates the glycaemic response of the added rose syrup sugar. Glycaemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (58 × 23.75) / 100 ≈ 13.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (45 mg/100 g) with beneficial potassium (140 mg/100 g) and calcium (115 mg/100 g) from milk
hypertension.guidance: Rose Milk is low in sodium and provides potassium and calcium that support blood pressure regulation; individuals with hypertension should use low-fat milk and verify that the rose syrup contains no added salt, and should moderate intake if using full-fat whole milk due to its saturated fat content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High added sugar from rose syrup (approximately 8.5 g sugar per 100 g, ~21 g per standard 250 ml serving) with a medium glycaemic index of 58 and glycaemic load of ~13.8 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit standard rose milk due to its high added refined sugar content; a sugar-free rose syrup alternative with low-fat milk can reduce the glycaemic impact, and blood glucose should be monitored if consumed.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from whole milk (~1.9 g/100 g) and high added sugar content contributing to caloric density and potential dyslipidaemia risk with frequent consumption
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit rose milk made with whole milk and high-sugar syrup; substituting low-fat or skimmed milk and a sugar-free syrup reduces saturated fat and caloric load, making occasional consumption more acceptable within a heart-healthy diet.

lactose_intolerance.key_factor: Primary ingredient is cow's milk containing lactose
lactose_intolerance.guidance: Individuals with lactose intolerance should substitute lactose-free milk or a plant-based milk alternative when preparing rose milk to avoid gastrointestinal discomfort.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rose Milk is a popular Indian chilled beverage prepared by mixing cold whole cow's milk with a sweetened, artificially or naturally flavoured rose syrup, resulting in a vibrant pink drink with a sweet, floral aroma and a smooth, creamy texture. It is consumed across all regions of India and is especially prominent in South Indian cities such as Chennai, Bengaluru, and Kochi, where it is a staple at home, school canteens, roadside stalls, and festive gatherings including weddings and religious celebrations. The drink is made without cooking — chilled milk is simply stirred or blended with a measured quantity of rose syrup (typically a 90:10 milk-to-syrup ratio). Some traditional versions add soaked sabja (sweet basil) seeds, which swell in the liquid and are believed to enhance the cooling effect. Nutritionally, rose milk provides approximately 78 kcal per 100 g, with 3.0 g protein, 3.2 g fat, and 9.5 g carbohydrate, of which 8.5 g is sugar. The milk component contributes meaningful calcium (115 mg/100 g), potassium (140 mg/100 g), riboflavin (0.15 mg/100 g), and vitamin B12 (0.4 mcg/100 g). The high added sugar content from the syrup gives it a medium glycaemic index of approximately 58 and a glycaemic load of ~13.8 per 250 ml serving, making it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes unless sugar-free syrup is used. It is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal-compatible, but contains dairy and is not suitable for those with lactose intolerance. Rose milk is best consumed as an occasional refreshing snack or festive beverage rather than a daily drink due to its added sugar content.
common_user_queries: How many calories in rose milk?
Is rose milk good for diabetes?
How to make rose milk at home?
What is rose milk made of?

confidence_reason: Rose Milk is not listed as a discrete entry in the NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods tables or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from NIN whole cow's milk data with an estimated rose syrup sugar contribution and cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy whole milk (FDC ID 1097512). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, cholesterol, and water values are estimated from the milk component using USDA SR Legacy data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for sweetened flavoured milk. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from NIN whole cow's milk data combined with an estimated rose syrup contribution (~10% v/v). No discrete rose milk entry exists in NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. USDA SR Legacy whole milk (FDC ID 1097512) used as cross-check for fat fractions, cholesterol, and water content. 2. Saturated fat (1.9 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.8 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.1 g), cholesterol_mg (13 mg), and water_g (83.5 g) are estimated from the milk component using USDA SR Legacy data and are not directly reported by NIN for this composite beverage. 3. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for plain rose milk without sabja seeds, consistent with NIN and USDA data for fluid whole milk with sugar syrup. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0: naturally negligible in this preparation; confirmed trace per USDA SR Legacy whole milk data. 5. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database sweetened flavoured milk entries (GI ~55–60); no direct GI measurement for rose milk exists in the published literature. Glycaemic load per 250 g serving = (58 × 23.75) / 100 ≈ 13.8. 6. Portions in the input JSON contained four identical duplicate entries; deduplicated to one standard serving of 250 ml (250 g). 7. food_group corrected from 'Snacks' to 'Beverages' as rose milk is a liquid drink. 8. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 (flavoured milk food product) and FoodEx2 A0FHR (flavoured milk drinks) used as closest defensible matches; no exact rose milk code exists in either system. 9. No -1 sentinel values require special justification beyond the standard template defaults for monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g, all of which have been populated with estimated values from approved sources.
